Developer Objective: Get platform access so the developer can work within the NEO ecosystem: LIAM permission package grants base roles, Snowflake account enables data access, and GitLab SSO enables code collaboration
Process Steps: Request LIAM permission package via iam.luminus.be (4me self-service request if needed). Verify Snowflake email address and confirm login. Confirm GitLab SSO access. Ensure all three access points are active before proceeding.
Main Output / Gate: Active LIAM roles, verified Snowflake login, and confirmed GitLab SSO access
Snowflake: Verify email address per NEO onboarding guide; confirm login and default role works
GitLab: SSO login confirmed; user appears in correct identity provider group

Developer Objective: Join an existing Purpose or request creation of a new one. Purposes define the PBAC (Purpose-Based Access Control) boundary — they determine which databases, schemas, and roles the developer can use.
Process Steps: Submit 4me self-service request → NEO Purpose Request. Purpose owner reviews and approves membership. PBAC roles are assigned to the developer. Purpose-specific Snowflake databases and schemas become available upon approval.
Main Output / Gate: Approved Purpose membership with assigned PBAC roles and accessible Purpose-specific Snowflake databases and schemas
Data Product Manager (DPM): Purpose exists or is created; developer roles assigned within the Purpose boundary
Snowflake: Purpose-specific databases (e.g., P_{PURPOSE}) and schemas become available after role assignment

Developer Objective: Get GitLab access, join the correct team group, and understand repository conventions (branching strategy, MR process, naming standards) so code contributions follow team standards
Process Steps: Confirm GitLab SSO login works. Request team group membership from team lead. Clone the starter/template repository. Review branching conventions (e.g., feature branches, MR-based merges). Familiarize with CI/CD pipeline structure in the repo.
Main Output / Gate: Working GitLab access with correct team group membership and understanding of repo conventions
GitLab: SSO login confirmed; joined team group; cloned starter repo; reviewed branching and MR conventions; understood CI/CD pipeline structure

Process Step: Set up Ona / VS Code Workspace
Developer Objective: Connect the cloud IDE (Ona or VS Code) to Snowflake and GitLab so the developer has a fully functional development environment for writing and testing SQL, YAML, and dbt models
Process Steps: Open Ona / VS Code workspace. Configure Snowflake connection (account, role, warehouse, database). Connect workspace to GitLab repository. Validate that SQL queries execute against the correct Snowflake environment. Ensure dbt commands (e.g., dbt run, dbt test) work locally.
Main Output / Gate: Working IDE workspace connected to Snowflake and GitLab with validated SQL execution and dbt commands
Snowflake: Validate active role, warehouse, compute, database, and target schemas respond correctly
Ona / VS Code: Open Ona / VS Code; configure Snowflake connection parameters; connect to GitLab repo; validate SQL execution and dbt commands

Developer Objective: Identify which existing SADPs, ADPs, or CADPs will serve as upstream inputs for the new CADP. These upstream products already exist and are published — the goal is to find the right ones and confirm they match the consumer use case.
Process Steps: Search the Data Product Portal catalog for relevant upstream data products. Compare candidate products by grain, refresh cadence, ownership, and coverage. Review metadata, descriptions, and data agreements. Shortlist the most suitable upstream products and note their owners and support routes.
Main Output / Gate: Shortlist of relevant upstream data products (SADPs/ADPs/CADPs) with known owner, purpose, grain, and refresh cadence
Data Product Portal: Search catalog; identify relevant upstream products; compare by grain, coverage, and refresh cadence
Data Product Manager (DPM): View owner, metadata, data agreements, and support route for each candidate
Snowflake: Upstream product schemas exist in public schemas but may not yet be accessible to the developer

Developer Objective: Obtain approved read access to the required upstream data product inputs. Access is governed by PBAC — the Purpose owner must approve, and roles are granted at the schema level.
Process Steps: Initiate access request from the Data Product Portal entry for each upstream product. Purpose owner reviews and approves the data agreement. PBAC roles granting read access to the upstream public schema are assigned. Verify read access works by querying the upstream tables in Snowflake.
Main Output / Gate: Approved data agreements and working read permissions to all required upstream public schemas
Data Product Portal: Initiate access request from the product entry for each upstream product
Data Product Manager (DPM): Manage approval workflow, data agreements, and metadata linkage
Snowflake: Apply PBAC roles and read access to upstream public schemas after approval; verify by querying upstream tables
GitLab: Track request references or linked implementation items if needed

Developer Objective: Profile the upstream data products in detail to understand their structure, semantics, grain, keys, refresh cadence, and any quirks before designing the CADP contract. This avoids surprises during build.
Process Steps: Review descriptions, refresh cadence, and usage guidance in the Portal. Inspect tables, columns, primary/foreign keys, and data types in Snowflake. Profile sample data: check for nulls, duplicates, cardinality, and edge cases. Assess join feasibility between multiple upstream products. Document findings and any data quality concerns.
Main Output / Gate: Initial source assessment: tables, columns, grain, refresh cadence, join feasibility, data quality notes, and usability assessment
Data Product Portal: Review description, refresh cadence, and usage guidance
Snowflake: Inspect tables, columns, keys, data types, and sample data in upstream public schemas
Ona / VS Code: Use connected workspace / SQL exploration to profile data; run exploratory queries for nulls, duplicates, cardinality
GitLab: Capture exploratory queries and profiling notes if useful

Developer Objective: Translate the consumer use case into a stable, well-documented CADP contract before heavy coding starts. Define the target grain, facts, dimensions, keys, naming conventions, test expectations, and publish intent.
Process Steps: Check the Portal for existing products to avoid duplication and align naming. Define the CADP grain (what does one row represent?). Identify facts (measures) and dimensions (attributes). Define primary keys, foreign keys, and naming conventions per CADP → BI Modeling Standards. Specify expected dbt tests (uniqueness, not_null, relationships, accepted_values). Document design decisions in ADRs or design notes. Plan the publish intent: what will the public schema expose?
Main Output / Gate: CADP design blueprint: grain, facts, dimensions, keys, naming conventions, test strategy, and publish intent documented
Data Product Portal: Check for existing products to avoid duplication and align naming
Data Product Manager (DPM): Define metadata requirements, ownership fields, and publication expectations
Snowflake: Analyse upstream structure, grain, keys, and feasible joins/measures
Ona / VS Code: Draft design notes, ERDs, and exploratory queries
GitLab: Store design artefacts, ADRs, and modelling decisions in the repo
Vulcan: Identify runtime and publish configuration needs (e.g., materialization strategy, schedule_type)
DBT: Define target models, facts, dimensions, tests, and materializations in dbt project structure

Developer Objective: Implement the CADP model layers (staging → intermediate → marts) that convert upstream inputs into governed, business-ready outputs following dbt and Vulcan conventions
Process Steps: Build staging models (stg_) that rename, cast, and clean upstream columns. Build intermediate models (int_) for complex joins, aggregations, and business logic. Build mart models (fct_ and dim_) that represent the final CADP contract. Configure materializations (view, table, incremental) per model. Add dbt tests (schema tests and custom tests) to each model. Iterate: run models in DEV schema (P_{PURPOSE}), inspect results, fix issues.
Main Output / Gate: Working DEV models producing intermediate and final CADP outputs in the P_{PURPOSE} schema with passing dbt tests
Snowflake: Execute models in DEV schema (P_{PURPOSE}) and persist outputs for iteration and inspection
Ona / VS Code: Write, run, and debug SQL, YAML, and dbt model config; iterate on model logic
GitLab: Version code changes continuously; commit frequently to feature branch
Vulcan: Define runtime configuration and publish behavior in Vulcan project files
GitLab CI/CD: Prepare automated checks for later deployment (e.g., CI pipeline validates on MR)
DBT: Build staging (stg_), intermediate (int_), fact (fct_), and dimension (dim_) models with tests and documentation

Developer Objective: Prove that the CADP output is correct, trustworthy, and fit for consumer use. Validate data quality, business logic, reconciliation against source, and performance before moving to deployment.
Process Steps: Run all dbt tests (uniqueness, not_null, relationships, accepted_values, custom). Validate data quality: check joins, duplicates, nulls, orphan keys, and row counts. Reconcile CADP output against upstream source totals where possible. Check query performance and materialization efficiency. Run Soda data contracts to validate quality gates. Fix any defects found and re-validate until all checks pass.
Main Output / Gate: Accepted DEV-quality output with all dbt tests passing, Soda contracts validated, reconciliation confirmed, and defects resolved
Data Product Manager (DPM): Support readiness for later publication if validation passes
Snowflake: Validate data quality, joins, duplicates, nulls, reconciliation, and query performance in DEV schema
Ona / VS Code: Debug queries and inspect unexpected results; run reconciliation queries
GitLab: Track fixes and reviewable changes in feature branch
Vulcan: Adjust runtime config if validation exposes issues (e.g., materialization changes)
GitLab CI/CD: Run branch-level validation where available (CI pipeline on MR)
DBT: Run dbt tests and semantic validations
Soda: Run Soda data contracts; validate quality checks; review results in Soda Cloud

Process Step: Register CADP in Data Product Manager
Developer Objective: Register the CADP in the Data Product Manager so that the public Snowflake schema (<purpose>__<data_id>) is provisioned. This must happen before CI/CD deployment can succeed, because the deployment target schema must exist.
Process Steps: Create a new data product entry in the Data Product Manager. Define the public schema name following the naming convention (<purpose>__<data_id>). Configure ownership, metadata, and support route. Trigger public schema provisioning in Snowflake. Confirm the public schema exists and is ready to receive data. Update the Vulcan project to reference the registered data product ID.
Main Output / Gate: Registered CADP with provisioned public schema (<purpose>__<data_id>) in Snowflake, referenced in Vulcan project config
Data Product Manager (DPM): Create data product entry; define public schema name; provision public schema; configure ownership and metadata
Snowflake: Public schema (<purpose>__<data_id>) provisioned and ready to receive published data
Vulcan: Vulcan project references registered data product ID in configuration

Developer Objective: Move approved CADP logic from the development branch into the controlled deployment flow. Code is merged, CI/CD validates and deploys, and the pipeline becomes executable in production.
Process Steps: Finalize code and configuration; ensure all tests pass on the feature branch. Create a Merge Request (MR) for peer review. Address review feedback and get MR approved. Merge code to main / approved branch. CI/CD pipeline triggers: validates, builds, and deploys to target environment. Verify deployment succeeded: check that pipeline definition and runtime config are in place.
Main Output / Gate: Deployed pipeline definition and executable production configuration with successful CI/CD run
Snowflake: Target environments receive deployed pipeline definitions and resulting objects
Ona / VS Code: Finalize code and configuration before release handoff
GitLab: Merge code to main / approved branch after MR review and approval
Vulcan: Provide runtime configuration for deployment (schedule, dependencies, parameters)
GitLab CI/CD: Deploy pipeline and configuration through automation; validate deployment success
Conveyor / Airflow: Register DAG / job execution definition in Conveyor/Airflow
DBT: Deployed dbt project ready for execution by pipeline

Developer Objective: Execute the CADP pipeline repeatedly so consumers receive refreshed outputs on the agreed cadence. Use event-based scheduling (schedule_type: events) to trigger when upstream data products update, ensuring freshness without unnecessary runs.
Process Steps: Configure schedule_type: events in Vulcan to trigger on upstream product refresh. Verify first production run completes successfully. Monitor subsequent runs for failures, performance degradation, or data quality issues. Set up alerting for failed runs. Handle reruns and backfills as needed.
Main Output / Gate: Successful recurring production runs with refreshed CADP data in the private schema (P_{PURPOSE}), monitored and alertable
Snowflake: Read upstream inputs from public schemas and write refreshed CADP outputs to private schema (P_{PURPOSE})
GitLab: Repository remains source of truth for deployed logic
Vulcan: Supply runtime parameters; configure schedule_type: events for event-based triggering on upstream refresh
Conveyor / Airflow: Schedule, trigger, monitor, and rerun jobs; handle backfills
DBT: Execute dbt transformation logic during each scheduled run
Soda: Validate output quality on each run via Soda data contracts

Developer Objective: Move validated data from the private schema (P_{PURPOSE}) to the public schema (<purpose>__<data_id>) using the Write-Audit-Publish (WAP) pattern. Soda data contracts gate the publish step — failed contracts block publication to protect consumers.
Process Steps: Data is written to private schema P_{PURPOSE} during the scheduled run (Write). Soda data contracts validate the output against defined quality thresholds (Audit). If all contracts pass: data is zero-copy cloned from private to public schema in a single atomic database transaction (Publish). If any contract fails: publication is blocked, alerting triggers, and the issue must be resolved before retry.
Main Output / Gate: Published CADP tables in public schema (<purpose>__<data_id>), validated by Soda data contracts, via atomic zero-copy clone
Snowflake: Data written to P_{PURPOSE} → validated by Soda contracts → zero-copy cloned to public schema (<purpose>__<data_id>) in single atomic transaction
Vulcan: WAP pattern configured in Vulcan project files (write, audit, publish steps)
GitLab CI/CD: CI/CD triggers WAP publish step after Soda validation passes
Soda: Soda data contracts gate the publish step; failed contracts block publication; results visible in Soda Cloud

Process Step: Register and Expose CADP for Consumers
Developer Objective: Make the finished CADP discoverable, documented, and consumable as an official governed data product. Consumers can find it in the Portal, understand its contents, and request access through the standard PBAC process.
Process Steps: Expose the CADP in the Data Product Portal for consumer discovery. Complete all metadata: description, owner, support route, refresh cadence, grain, key columns, and usage guidance. Define the access request and grant process (PBAC-based). Optionally store versioned documentation alongside code in GitLab. Announce availability to relevant consumer teams.
Main Output / Gate: Published CADP with complete discoverable metadata, governed access path, and consumer-ready documentation
Data Product Portal: Expose CADP to consumers for discovery and access requests
Data Product Manager (DPM): Register CADP metadata, ownership, support route, refresh cadence, and grant process
Snowflake: Host final CADP tables / views in public schema for consumption
GitLab: Optionally store versioned documentation alongside code
Vulcan: May support publish-specific configuration if implemented
DBT: Published dbt-built objects back the governed consumer-ready product
